name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
إِنَّ شَرَّ ٱلدَّوَآبِّ عِندَ ٱللَّهِ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ فَهُمْ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ (٥٥)
```

```english
Indeed the worst of beasts in Allah’s sight are those who are faithless; so they will not have faith. (55)
```

```arabic
ٱلَّذِينَ عَـٰهَدتَّ مِنْهُمْ ثُمَّ يَنقُضُونَ عَهْدَهُمْ فِى كُلِّ مَرَّةٍ وَهُمْ لَا يَتَّقُونَ (٥٦)
```

```english
—Those with whom you made a treaty, and who violated their treaty every time, and who are not Godwary. (56)
```

```arabic
فَإِمَّا تَثْقَفَنَّهُمْ فِى ٱلْحَرْبِ فَشَرِّدْ بِهِم مَّنْ خَلْفَهُمْ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَذَّكَّرُونَ (٥٧)
```

```english
So if you confront them in battle, treat them [in such a wise] as to disperse those who are behind them, so that they may take admonition. (57)
```

```arabic
وَإِمَّا تَخَافَنَّ مِن قَوْمٍ خِيَانَةً فَٱنۢبِذْ إِلَيْهِمْ عَلَىٰ سَوَآءٍ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يُحِبُّ ٱلْخَآئِنِينَ (٥٨)
```

```english
And if you fear treachery from a people, break off [the treaty] with them in a like manner. Indeed Allah does not like the treacherous. (58)
```

```arabic
وَلَا يَحْسَبَنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ سَبَقُوٓا۟ ۚ إِنَّهُمْ لَا يُعْجِزُونَ (٥٩)
```

```english
Let the faithless not suppose that they have outmanoeuvred [Allah]. Indeed, they cannot frustrate [His power]. (59)
```

```arabic
وَأَعِدُّوا۟ لَهُم مَّا ٱسْتَطَعْتُم مِّن قُوَّةٍ وَمِن رِّبَاطِ ٱلْخَيْلِ تُرْهِبُونَ بِهِۦ عَدُوَّ ٱللَّهِ وَعَدُوَّكُمْ وَءَاخَرِينَ مِن دُونِهِمْ لَا تَعْلَمُونَهُمُ ٱللَّهُ يَعْلَمُهُمْ ۚ وَمَا تُنفِقُوا۟ مِن شَىْءٍ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ يُوَفَّ إِلَيْكُمْ وَأَنتُمْ لَا تُظْلَمُونَ (٦٠)
```

```english
Prepare against them whatever you can of [military] power and war-horses, awing thereby the enemy of Allah, and your enemy, and others besides them, whom you do not know, but Allah knows them. And whatever you spend in the way of Allah will be repaid to you in full, and you will not be wronged. (60)
```

```arabic
۞ وَإِن جَنَحُوا۟ لِلسَّلْمِ فَٱجْنَحْ لَهَا وَتَوَكَّلْ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ هُوَ ٱلسَّمِيعُ ٱلْعَلِيمُ (٦١)
```

```english
If they incline toward peace, then you [too] incline toward it, and put your trust in Allah. Indeed He is the All-hearing, the All-knowing. (61)
```

```arabic
وَإِن يُرِيدُوٓا۟ أَن يَخْدَعُوكَ فَإِنَّ حَسْبَكَ ٱللَّهُ ۚ هُوَ ٱلَّذِىٓ أَيَّدَكَ بِنَصْرِهِۦ وَبِٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (٦٢)
```

```english
But if they desire to deceive you, Allah is indeed sufficient for you. It is He who strengthened you with His help and with the means of the faithful, (62)
```

```arabic
وَأَلَّفَ بَيْنَ قُلُوبِهِمْ ۚ لَوْ أَنفَقْتَ مَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ جَمِيعًا مَّآ أَلَّفْتَ بَيْنَ قُلُوبِهِمْ وَلَـٰكِنَّ ٱللَّهَ أَلَّفَ بَيْنَهُمْ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ عَزِيزٌ حَكِيمٌ (٦٣)
```

```english
and united their hearts. Had you spent all that is in the earth, you could not have united their hearts, but Allah united them together. Indeed He is all-mighty, all-wise. (63)
```

```arabic
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّبِىُّ حَسْبُكَ ٱللَّهُ وَمَنِ ٱتَّبَعَكَ مِنَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (٦٤)
```

```english
O Prophet! Sufficient for you is Allah and those of the faithful who follow you. (64)
```

```arabic
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّبِىُّ حَرِّضِ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ عَلَى ٱلْقِتَالِ ۚ إِن يَكُن مِّنكُمْ عِشْرُونَ صَـٰبِرُونَ يَغْلِبُوا۟ مِا۟ئَتَيْنِ ۚ وَإِن يَكُن مِّنكُم مِّا۟ئَةٌ يَغْلِبُوٓا۟ أَلْفًا مِّنَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ بِأَنَّهُمْ قَوْمٌ لَّا يَفْقَهُونَ (٦٥)
```

```english
O Prophet! Urge on the faithful to fight: If there be twenty patient men among you, they shall overcome two hundred; and if there be a hundred of you, they shall overcome a thousand of the faithless, for they are a lot who do not understand. (65)
```

```arabic
ٱلْـَٔـٰنَ خَفَّفَ ٱللَّهُ عَنكُمْ وَعَلِمَ أَنَّ فِيكُمْ ضَعْفًا ۚ فَإِن يَكُن مِّنكُم مِّا۟ئَةٌ صَابِرَةٌ يَغْلِبُوا۟ مِا۟ئَتَيْنِ ۚ وَإِن يَكُن مِّنكُمْ أَلْفٌ يَغْلِبُوٓا۟ أَلْفَيْنِ بِإِذْنِ ٱللَّهِ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ مَعَ ٱلصَّـٰبِرِينَ (٦٦)
```

```english
Now Allah has lightened your burden, knowing that there is weakness in you. So if there be a hundred patient men among you, they shall overcome two hundred; and if there be a thousand, they shall overcome two thousand, by Allah’s leave; and Allah is with the patient. (66)
```

## (Commentary)

These verses contain rulings and statutes concerning war, peace, treaties, and their revocation, among other matters. The opening of these verses is applicable to the Jewish tribes that were in and around Medina. The Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) had entered into a treaty with them after his migration to Medina, stipulating that they would not harm him, betray him, or aid an enemy against him, while they would be allowed to remain upon their religion and be secure in their persons. However, they broke the covenant (العهد) — *the binding agreement and solemn pledge* — time after time, until Allah, the Glorified, commanded that they be fought. Their affair ended as it did, and some accounts of them will follow in the upcoming "Discussion of Narrations," if Allah, the Exalted, wills.

Based on this, the first four verses were not revealed alongside the preceding verses, nor are they connected to them, as indicated by their context (السياق). As for the remaining seven, their connection to the preceding four verses, or to those before them, is not clearly evident.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «إِنَّ شَرَّ الدَّوَابِّ عِنْدَ اللهِ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا فَهُمْ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ» **"Indeed the worst of beasts in Allah’s sight are those who are faithless, so they will not believe."** (8:55). This discourse is aimed at clarifying that these individuals are undoubtedly the worst of all living creatures. This is indicated by the qualification of the judgment with the phrase "in Allah’s sight" (عِنْدَ اللهِ), for it signifies His judgment; and that which Allah, the Glorified, judges and decrees is not subject to error. As the Exalted has said: «لا يَضِلُّ رَبِّي وَلا يَنْسى» **"My Lord neither errs nor forgets."** (20:52).

He opened this segment of the discourse concerning them by stating they are the "worst of beasts" in His sight because the objective of the speech is to warn against them and repel them. It is ingrained in natural dispositions (الطباع) that evil (الشر) from which no good is expected must be repelled by any valid and possible means. Thus, it was appropriate to open with the clarification that they are the worst of beasts, in preparation for what He would later command regarding them in His saying: «فَإِمَّا تَثْقَفَنَّهُمْ فِي الْحَرْبِ فَشَرِّدْ بِهِمْ مَنْ خَلْفَهُمْ» **"If you confront them in war, then scatter those who are behind them by [making an example of] them..."** (8:57).

He followed His saying, "those who are faithless" (الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا), with His saying, "so they will not believe" (فَهُمْ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ), beginning with the "fa" of derivation (فاء التفريع). This means that among their attributes, which stems from their disbelief, is that they will not believe. Faithlessness (عدم الإيمان) only stems from disbelief (الكفر) when the latter becomes so deeply rooted in the soul that its removal is no longer expected. At that point, there is no hope for faith (الإيمان) to enter a heart of this nature, due to the mutual opposition (المضادة) between disbelief and faith.

From this, it becomes clear that the intended meaning of "those who are faithless" (الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا) refers to those who have become fixed in their disbelief. Consequently, the meaning of this verse returns to its earlier counterpart: «إِنَّ شَرَّ الدَّوَابِّ عِنْدَ اللهِ الصُّمُّ الْبُكْمُ الَّذِينَ لا يَعْقِلُونَ وَلَوْ عَلِمَ اللهُ فِيهِمْ خَيْراً لَأَسْمَعَهُمْ وَلَوْ أَسْمَعَهُمْ لَتَوَلَّوْا وَهُمْ مُعْرِضُونَ» **"Indeed the worst of beasts in Allah’s sight are the deaf and the dumb who do not exercise their reason. Had Allah known any good in them, He would have surely made them hear, and were He to make them hear, they would surely have turned away, being disregardful."** (8:22-23).

Furthermore, since both verses indicate the restriction of "evil" in the sight of Allah to a specific group of beasts, the first verse—while indicating that its subjects are those who will never believe—serves as evidence that the intended meaning of "those who are faithless, so they will not believe" in the second verse is that they are fixed in their disbelief and will never deviate from it.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «الَّذِينَ عاهَدْتَ مِنْهُمْ ثُمَّ يَنْقُضُونَ عَهْدَهُمْ فِي كُلِّ مَرَّةٍ وَهُمْ لا يَتَّقُونَ» **"Those with whom you made a treaty, and who then break their treaty every time, and who are not God-conscious."** (8:56). This is an elucidation (بيان) of "those who are faithless" in the previous verse, or a substitution (بدل) for them—specifically a "substitution of the part for the whole" (بدل البعض من الكل). It follows from this that the word "from" (من) in His saying "from them" (مِنْهُمْ) is partitive (تبعيضية). The meaning is: those with whom you made a treaty from among those who are faithless. As for the possibility that "from" is redundant (زائدة), meaning "those whom you made a treaty with," or that it means "with" (مع), meaning "those with whom you made a treaty," these are of no consequence.

The phrase "every time" (كُلِّ مَرَّةٍ) refers to the instances of treaty-making; that is, they break their covenant every time you enter into a treaty with them. "And they are not God-conscious" (وَهُمْ لا يَتَّقُونَ) means they do not fear Allah regarding the breaking of the covenant, or they do not fear you and do not dread the consequences of breaking your treaty. This indicates their repeated violation of agreements.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «فَإِمَّا تَثْقَفَنَّهُمْ فِي الْحَرْبِ فَشَرِّدْ بِهِمْ مَنْ خَلْفَهُمْ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَذَّكَّرُونَ» **"If you confront them in war, then scatter those who are behind them by [making an example of] them, so that they may take admonition."** (8:57).

It is stated in *al-Majma‘*: **al-Thaqf** (الثقف) means to overcome and apprehend with speed, and **al-Tashrid** (التشريد) means to disperse in a state of agitation. End quote. His saying: «فَإِمَّا تَثْقَفَنَّهُمْ» is originally "in tathqafhum" (إن تثقفهم), where the emphatic "ma" (ما) was added to the conditional "in" (إن) to justify the entry of the emphatic "nun" (نون التأكيد) upon the condition. The discourse is framed for emphasis within the conditional structure.

The meaning of "scattering those who are behind them by [making an example of] them" is to inflict such exemplary punishment (التنكيل) and severity upon them that those behind them take heed. Thus, terror and fear will seize their hearts, causing them to disperse, and the bond of their resolve and the unity of their will to fight the believers and nullify the word of truth will be dissolved.

Accordingly, the meaning of His saying: "so that they may take admonition" (لَعَلَّهُمْ يَذَّكَّرُونَ) is the hope that they might remember the evil consequences and the ill-fated end of breaking covenants, spreading corruption in the land, and opposing the word of truth; for Allah does not guide the transgressing people, nor does He guide the schemes of the treacherous.

The verse contains an allusion to the command to fight them, and then to deal with them severely and make an example of them when they are overcome and apprehended. It also alludes to the fact that behind them are those whose condition is like theirs in breaking covenants and waiting for misfortunes to befall the truth and its people.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «وَإِمَّا تَخافَنَّ مِنْ قَوْمٍ خِيانَةً فَانْبِذْ إِلَيْهِمْ عَلى سَواءٍ إِنَّ اللهَ لا يُحِبُّ الْخائِنِينَ» **"And if you fear treachery from a people, break off [the treaty] with them in a like manner. Indeed Allah does not like the treacherous."** (8:58). **Treachery** (الخيانة) — according to *al-Majma‘* — is the breaking of a covenant in that which one is entrusted with. This is the meaning of treachery regarding covenants and pacts. As for treachery in its general sense, it is the breaking of what has been firmly established of truth in a covenant or a trust. **al-Nabdh** (النبذ) is to cast away, as in His saying: «فَنَبَذُوهُ وَراءَ ظُهُورِهِمْ» **"they cast it behind their backs"** (3:187). **al-Sawa’** (السواء) is in the sense of equality (الاستواء) and justice (العدل).

His saying: «وَإِمَّا تَخافَنَ» is like His saying in the previous verse: «فَإِمَّا تَثْقَفَنَّهُمْ». The meaning of "fear" (الخوف) here is the appearance of signs (أمارات) indicating the occurrence of that which must be guarded against and cautioned. His saying: "Indeed Allah does not like the treacherous" (إِنَّ اللهَ لا يُحِبُّ الْخائِنِينَ) is the justification (تعليل) for His saying: "break off [the treaty] with them in a like manner."

The meaning of the verse is: If you fear from a people with whom there is a treaty that they will betray you and break their covenant, and signs indicating this have appeared, then cast back and return their covenant to them. Inform them of the cancellation of the treaty so that you and they are on equal footing regarding the revocation of the covenant, or so that you are established upon justice. For it is justice to treat others in kind and with equality (السواء); because if you were to fight them before canceling the treaty, that would be treachery on your part, and Allah does not love the treacherous.

The summary of the two verses provides two divine statutes (دستوران) regarding fighting those who have no regard for covenants, either due to their actual violation or the fear of it. If the people of the treaty from among the disbelievers do not remain firm upon their covenant, breaking it every time, then the guardian of the affair (ولي الأمر) must fight them and deal with them severely. If they are such that their treachery is feared and their covenant cannot be trusted, they are to be informed of the cancellation of their treaty and then fought. One must not begin fighting them before informing them, for that is treachery. However, if they have made a treaty and have not broken it, nor is their treachery feared, then it is obligatory to maintain their treaty and respect their contract. As the Exalted has said: «فَأَتِمُّوا إِلَيْهِمْ عَهْدَهُمْ إِلى مُدَّتِهِمْ» **"so fulfill their treaty for them until [the end of] their term"** (9:4), and He said: «أَوْفُوا بِالْعُقُودِ» **"fulfill your biddings"** (5:1).

**His saying, the Exalted:** «وَلا يَحْسَبَنَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا سَبَقُوا إِنَّهُمْ لا يُعْجِزُونَ» **"Let the faithless not suppose that they have outmaneuvered [Us]. Indeed they cannot frustrate [Allah]."** (8:59). The popular recitation is "tahsabanna" (تحسبن) with the "ta" of address, which is an address to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to comfort his soul and strengthen his heart, similar to the address coming after several verses: «يا أَيُّهَا النَّبِيُّ حَسْبُكَ اللهُ وَمَنِ اتَّبَعَكَ مِنَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ» **"O Prophet! Allah is sufficient for you and those who follow you of the faithful"** (8:64), and like the address delivered after it to incite the believers: «يا أَيُّهَا النَّبِيُّ حَرِّضِ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ عَلَى الْقِتالِ» **"O Prophet! Rouse the faithful to fighting..."** (8:65).

"Outmaneuvering" (السبق) is for a thing to get ahead of the one seeking to catch it. **al-I‘jaz** (الإعجاز) is to cause incapacity (العجز). His saying: "Indeed they cannot frustrate [Allah]" (إِنَّهُمْ لا يُعْعِزُونَ) is the justification for His saying: "Let [them] not suppose..." etc. The meaning is: O Prophet, do not suppose that those who are faithless have outmaneuvered Us such that We cannot overtake them, for they cannot frustrate Allah, and He has power over all things.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «وَأَعِدُّوا لَهُمْ مَا اسْتَطَعْتُمْ مِنْ قُوَّةٍ وَمِنْ رِباطِ الْخَيْلِ» **"Prepare against them whatever you can of [military] power and war-horses..."** (8:60) to the end of the verse. **al-I‘dad** (الإعداد) is to prepare a thing to achieve something else, and to bring into existence what the desired thing needs for its realization, such as preparing wood and fuel for lighting a fire, and preparing the fire for cooking. **Power** (القوة) is everything with which an action can be performed. In war, it refers to everything by which war and defense are carried out, including types of weaponry, trained men, and military institutions that manage all such interests. **al-Ribat** (الرباط) is an intensive form of "al-rabt" (الربط), which is simpler than "al-‘aqd" (العقد). It is said: "rabatahu yarbituhu rabtan" and "rabatahu yurabituhu murabatan wa ribatan." All are in the same sense, except that *al-ribat* is more emphatic than *al-rabt*. **al-Khayl** (الخيل) refers to horses. **al-Irhab** (الإرهاب) is close in meaning to "frightening" (التخويف).

His saying: "Prepare against them whatever you can of [military] power and war-horses" is a general command to the believers to prepare, to the extent of their ability, the military forces they need against their existing or potential enemies. For human society is never free from being composed of individuals or peoples of differing natures and opposing ideas. No society can be established upon a valuable way of life (سنة قيمة) that benefits them without there being another society that opposes its interests and differs from its way of life. They cannot live together for a period of time without conflict breaking out between them, leading to dominance and subjugation.

Thus, devastating wars and the conflicts that lead to them are unavoidable in human societies—and societies are as they are. This is evidenced by what we observe in the creation of man, being equipped with faculties that are only utilized for defense, such as anger (الغضب), physical strength, and the intellect that works toward subjugation and dominance. Therefore, it is a primordial obligation (واجب فطري) upon the Islamic society to always be equipped by preparing whatever power and war-horses it can, according to the enemy it anticipates for its righteous society.

That which Allah has chosen for the Islamic society—through the primordial religion (الدين الفطري) He revealed to them, which is the "upright religion" (الدين القيم)—is the "human government" (الحكومة الإنسانية). In this government, the rights of every individual member of its society are preserved, and the interests of the weak and the strong, the rich and the poor, the free and the slave, the man and the woman, the individual and the group, the part and the whole, are observed equally. This is unlike an individualistic, autocratic government (الحكومة الفردية الاستبدادية) that operates only according to the whims of the individual in charge, who rules over people's lives, honors, and wealth as he pleases. Nor is it like a majoritarian government (الحكومة الأكثرية) that conforms to the whims of the masses, nullifying the interests of others, satisfying the majority (50% + 1) while oppressing and angering the minority (50% - 1).

Perhaps this is the secret behind His saying, the Exalted: "Prepare against them whatever you can of [military] power," where the address is directed to the people, whereas the address in the previous verses was directed to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), such as His saying: "If you confront them in war, then scatter those who are behind them," and His saying: "break off [the treaty] with them in a like manner," and His saying: "Let the faithless not suppose that they have outmaneuvered [Us]." This is also the case in the subsequent verses, such as His saying: "And if they incline toward peace, then you [too] incline toward it," among others.

This is because the Islamic government is a human government in the sense of observing the rights of every individual, magnifying the will of the few, and respecting the side of whoever it may be, without the effective will being restricted to one individual or the majority of individuals.

Therefore, the interests threatened by their enemy are the interests of every individual. Thus, every individual must stand to defend them and prepare whatever power he can to protect them from loss. Although some forms of preparation can only be undertaken by governments with their potent capabilities and vast resources, other forms can be undertaken by individuals in their individual capacity, such as learning military sciences and training in its arts. Thus, the obligation is an obligation upon everyone.

His saying, the Exalted: «تُرْهِبُونَ بِهِ عَدُوَّ اللهِ وَعَدُوَّكُمْ وَآخَرِينَ مِنْ دُونِهِمْ لا تَعْلَمُونَهُمُ اللهُ يَعْلَمُهُمْ» **"to deter thereby Allah’s enemy and your enemy, and others besides them whom you do not know, [but] Allah knows them."** (8:60). This is in the position of justification (تعليل) for His saying: "And prepare against them." That is, prepare that for them so that you may deter and frighten thereby the enemy of Allah and your enemy. In counting them as enemies of Allah and of them all, there is a statement of reality and an emphasis on incitement.

In His saying: "and others besides them whom you do not know," there is an indication that the first group refers to those whom the believers recognize as enemies to Allah and to themselves. The second group, whom the believers do not know—as suggested by the generality of the expression—refers to everyone whose threat of enmity the believers have no knowledge of. This includes the hypocrites (المنافقون) who are in the garb and form of the believers, outwardly praying, fasting, performing pilgrimage, and struggling (يجاهدون), as well as those from among the disbelievers whom the believers have not yet encountered.

Deterrence (الإرهاب) through the preparation of power, while being a valid objective in itself from which great and manifest benefits derive, is not the entirety of the intended purpose of preparing power. For this reason, He followed it with His saying: «وَما تُنْفِقُوا مِنْ شَيْءٍ فِي سَبِيلِ اللهِ يُوَفَّ إِلَيْكُمْ وَأَنْتُمْ لا تُظْلَمُونَ» **"Whatever you spend in the way of Allah will be repaid to you in full, and you will not be wronged."** (8:60), to indicate the comprehensive purpose.

This is because the true purpose of preparing forces is the ability to repel to the extent of one's capacity, and to protect the society from the enemy that threatens its lives, honors, and wealth. In terms appropriate to the purpose of religion, it is to extinguish the fire of corruption (الفساد) that nullifies the word of truth and destroys the foundation of the primordial religion (دين الفطرة) — *the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being* — through which Allah is worshipped in His earth and the standard of justice is established among His servants.

This is a matter from which every individual of the religious society benefits. Thus, whatever an individual or a group spends in the way of Allah—which is the struggle (الجهاد) to revive His cause—returns to the person himself, even if in another form. If one spends wealth, status, or any such blessing in His way, it is an expenditure in the way of necessities, the benefit of which soon returns to him, followed by growth in this world and the Hereafter. If one spends his life in His way, it is martyrdom (الشهادة) in the way of Allah, which entails a true, eternal, and everlasting life. For the like of this should the workers work—not like those individuals who sacrifice themselves for worldly goals for the sake of the survival of a name, the eternity of mention, and the completion of pride. Although such people have today become aware of this Islamic teaching—that society is like a single soul whose members share in the benefit and harm that returns to it—they have stumbled in their path. The matter has become confused for them in identifying the human perfection for which the primordial nature (الفطرة) summons and calls toward society, which is the enjoyment of eternal life. They supposed it to be the revolving worldly life, so the path became narrow for them in matters such as sacrificing the soul for the sake of others' enjoyment of material pleasures.

In short, the preparation of power is for the purpose of defending the rights of the Islamic society and its vital interests. The display of prepared power results in the deterrence of the enemy, which is also a branch of defense and a type of it. Thus, His saying, the Exalted: "to deter thereby Allah’s enemy..." etc., mentions one of the benefits of preparation that returns to the individuals of the society. His saying: "Whatever you spend in the way of Allah will be repaid to you in full, and you will not be wronged," mentions that what they spend in His way is not nullified or lost, but rather returns to them without any rightful person losing his right.

This—meaning His saying: "Whatever you spend in the way of Allah..." etc.—is more general in benefit than His saying: «وَما تُنْفِقُوا مِنْ خَيْرٍ يُوَفَّ إِلَيْكُمْ» **"And whatever good you spend will be repaid to you in full"** (2:272). For "good" (الخير) is typically understood as wealth, so it does not include the soul, unlike His saying here: "Whatever you spend of anything" (مِنْ شَيْءٍ).

**His saying, the Exalted:** «وَإِنْ جَنَحُوا لِلسَّلْمِ فَاجْنَحْ لَها وَتَوَكَّلْ عَلَى اللهِ إِنَّهُ هُوَ السَّمِيعُ الْعَلِيمُ» **"And if they incline toward peace, then you [too] incline toward it, and put your trust in Allah. Indeed He is the All-hearing, the All-knowing."** (8:61). In *al-Majma‘*: **al-Junuh** (الجنوح) is inclination (الميل). From it comes the wing (جناح) of a bird because it inclines with it to one of its sides. "There is no sin (جناح) upon him" means there is no inclination toward a sin. End quote. **al-Silm** (السلم), with either a *fatha* or *kasra* on the *sin*, means peace (الصلح).

His saying: "and put your trust in Allah" (وَتَوَكَّلْ عَلَى اللهِ) is part of the completion of the command to incline; thus, all are in the sense of a single command. The meaning is: If they incline toward peace and reconciliation, then incline toward it and rely in that upon Allah. Do not fear that causes hidden from you might overwhelm you during a moment of heedlessness or lack of preparation, for Allah is the All-hearing, the All-knowing. No cause escapes Him, and no plot frustrates Him; rather, He will help you and suffice you. This is what is affirmed by His saying in the following verse: «وَإِنْ يُرِيدُوا أَنْ يَخْدَعُوكَ فَإِنَّ حَسْبَكَ اللهُ» **"And if they intend to deceive you, then Allah is indeed sufficient for you."** (8:62).

It has already been mentioned in our previous discussion on the meaning of trust in Allah (التوكل على الله) that it is not reliance upon Him, the Glorified, by nullifying apparent causes. Rather, it is the removal of absolute reliance upon apparent causes, because what appears to man of them is only a small portion, not all of them. The complete cause (السبب التام) that never fails its effect is the totality that carries His will, the Glorified.

Thus, trust (التوكل) is the directing of confidence and reliance toward Allah, the Glorified, by Whose will the millstone of all causes turns. This does not contradict the one who trusts using whatever apparent causes are available to him, without nullifying any of them and thus riding the mount of ignorance.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «وَإِنْ يُرِيدُوا أَنْ يَخْدَعُوكَ فَإِنَّ حَسْبَكَ اللهُ هُوَ الَّذِي أَيَّدَكَ بِنَصْرِهِ وَبِالْمُؤْمِنِينَ» **"And if they intend to deceive you, then Allah is indeed sufficient for you. It is He who supported you with His help and with the faithful."** (8:62). This verse is connected to the preceding one and serves as a rebuttal to a potential objection. That is, when Allah, the Glorified, commanded His Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to incline toward peace if they incline toward it, and He did not approve of deception—because it is a form of treachery (الخيانة) against the rights of general social interaction and connection, and Allah does not love the treacherous—His command to incline as mentioned was a likely subject of a question. The question being: it is possible that their inclination toward peace is a deception by which they mislead the believers to attack them under suitable conditions. So He, the Glorified, answered: We have commanded you to trust (التوكل); so if they intend to deceive you by that, then Allah is sufficient for you. As the Exalted has said: «وَمَنْ يَتَوَكَّلْ عَلَى اللهِ فَهو حَسْبُهُ إِنَّ اللهَ بالِغُ أَمْرِهِ» **"And whoever puts his trust in Allah, He will suffice him. Indeed Allah carries through His command."** (65:3).

This is among the things that indicate there are causes beyond the natural, ordinary causes revealed to us, which proceed according to what suits the welfare of the trusting servant when the natural, ordinary causes fail him and do not assist him in his rightful objective.

His saying: "It is He who supported you with His help and with the faithful" is like an argument for His saying: "then Allah is indeed sufficient for you," by mentioning evidences that indicate His sufficiency, the Exalted. These are that He supported him with His help, supported him with the believers, and joined their hearts together when they were mutually hateful.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «وَأَلَّفَ بَيْنَ قُلُوبِهِمْ لَوْ أَنْفَقْتَ ما فِي الْأَرْضِ جَمِيعاً ما أَلَّفْتَ بَيْنَ قُلُوبِهِمْ وَلكِنَّ اللهَ أَلَّفَ بَيْنَهُمْ» **"and He united their hearts. Had you spent all that is in the earth, you could not have united their hearts, but Allah united them..."** (8:63) etc. Al-Raghib said: **al-Ilf** (الإلف) is a gathering with harmony (التيام). It is said: "allaftu baynahum" (I united them), and from it comes "al-ulfah" (affection/unity). The one who is familiar is called "ilf" and "alif." The Exalted said: «إِذْ كُنْتُمْ أَعْداءً فَأَلَّفَ بَيْنَ قُلُوبِكُمْ» **"when you were enemies, and He united your hearts"** (3:103). End quote.

He, the Glorified, cited among the evidences for His sufficiency for the one who trusts in Him that He sufficed His Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) by uniting the hearts of the believers, after mentioning His support for him through them. The discourse is absolute, and the criterion mentioned therein is general, encompassing all believers. However, the verse is more clearly applicable to the Ansar (الأنصار), through whom Allah supported His Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), so they sheltered him and helped him. Allah, the Glorified, through His religion, united them among themselves, after devastating wars had broken out among them and had been ongoing for a long time—namely the war of "Bu‘ath" between the Aws and the Khazraj—until they reconciled with the arrival of Islam in their land and became, by His grace, brothers.

Allah has reminded of His favor in uniting the hearts of the Muslims in several places in His speech, and He clarified the importance of its position with the like of His saying: "Had you spent all that is in the earth, you could not have united their hearts, but Allah united them. Indeed He is All-mighty, All-wise."

This is because man is created with an innate love for the vital blessings through which his life is completed; he has no goal besides them. In reality, he does not desire or intend anything except to benefit himself. Even what might appear as him desiring a benefit returning to another, careful reflection reveals it contains a benefit returning to himself. Since he loves attainment (الوجدان), he hates loss (الفقدان).

By these two instinctive attributes—namely love and hate—the matter of life is completed for him. If he were to love everything, including opposites and contradictions, life would be nullified. If he were to hate everything, even the mutually exclusive, life would be nullified. Allah, the Glorified, has created him with a social nature (الحياة الاجتماعية) because the powers and tools he possesses are insufficient to provide all the necessities of his life. It is necessary that society cannot be completed except by each individual specializing in that which others are deprived of—be it wealth, status, adornment, beauty, or anything over which human natures compete or to which psychological whims (الهوى النفساني) are attached, with variations in increase and decrease.

This is the first thing that deposits types of enmity and hatred in the hearts and stinginess (الشح) in the souls. Then, the various forms of deprivation that spread among them through injustice (الظلم), aggression (العدوان), and the transgression of some against others in blood, honor, wealth, or other things they enjoy, compete for, and work for, stir up all hatred and animosity within their souls.

All of these are internal attributes and instincts in the group that soon manifest in their actions and meet in their deeds, touching one another in the course of their lives. Therein lies the trial that is followed by social tribulations and calamities that destroy souls and perish crops and progeny. This has been witnessed by the events occurring over successive centuries and generations.

As long as the united nations think that their goal in their gathering is to enjoy a material life limited to the worldly life, there is no way to uproot the matter of this corruption from its source or cut off its origins. For this world is the abode of competition (التزاحم), and society is established on the basis of specialization (الاختصاص). Souls differ in aptitude, and the occurring events, effective factors, and external conditions are involved in their livelihoods and lives.

The Exalted said: «إِنَّ الْإِنْسانَ خُلِقَ هَلُوعاً إِذا مَسَّهُ الشَّرُّ جَزُوعاً وَإِذا مَسَّهُ الْخَيْرُ مَنُوعاً» **"Indeed man was created greedy: anxious when an ill befalls him, and grudging when good comes his way"** (70:19-21). And He said: «إِنَّ النَّفْسَ لأَمَّارَةٌ بِالسُّوءِ» **"the [carnal] soul indeed incites to evil"** (12:53). And He said: «وَلا يَزالُونَ مُخْتَلِفِينَ إِلَّا مَنْ رَحِمَ رَبُّكَ وَلِذلِكَ خَلَقَهُمْ» **"they will not cease to differ, except those on whom your Lord has mercy—and for that He created them"** (11:118-119), among other verses.

The most that man can do in spreading affection and satisfying hearts filled with enmity and hatred is to convince them or silence them by granting what they love of wealth, status, or other worldly blessings beloved to them. However, this only benefits in specific, particular cases. As for general enmity and hatred, there is no way to remove them from the hearts by granting blessings, for that does not nullify the instinct for more (الاستزادة) and the stinginess (الشح) burning in every soul due to the vital advantages observed in others.

Furthermore, some blessings only accept specialization and exclusivity, such as sovereignty (الملك) and high leadership (الرئاسة), and other matters that follow their course. Even advanced nations with civilization and culture have not been able to treat this disease except by that which removes some of its severity, allowing the body of society to rest from some of its torment. As for the hatreds related to matters that are exclusive to some of their society, such as leadership and sovereignty, they remain as they are, their sparks igniting the hearts, and they continue to consume one another.

Moreover, that is restricted to what is among them. As for societies outside of their own, their condition is not cared for, and no attention is paid to their vital interests except what conforms to the interests of the former, even if the calamities of misfortune exhaust them and time wears them out with hardship.

Allah has bestowed favor upon the Islamic nation by removing stinginess from their souls and uniting their hearts through a divine gnosis (معرفة إلهية) He taught them and spread among them. This was by clarifying that human life is an eternal life, not restricted to these few days that will perish while man remains, with no news of them. And that the happiness of this eternal life is not the enjoyment of material pleasures and grazing in the pastures of meanness. Rather, it is a real life and a true existence in which man lives and exists in the dignity of the servitude (العبودية) of Allah, the Glorified. He enjoys the blessings of proximity and nearness, then enjoys what is facilitated for him of the provisions of the worldly life that luck or acquisition has brought to him, recognizing the rights of the blessing. Then he moves to the proximity of Allah and enters the abode of His pleasure, mixing there with the righteous among His servants, and living the true life. The Exalted said: «وَمَا الْحَياةُ الدُّنْيا فِي الْآخِرَةِ إِلَّا مَتاعٌ» **"The life of this world is but a [fleeting] enjoyment compared with the Hereafter."** (13:26). And the Exalted said: «وَما هذِهِ الْحَياةُ الدُّنْيا إِلَّا لَهْوٌ وَلَعِبٌ وَإِنَّ الدَّارَ الْآخِرَةَ لَهِيَ الْحَيَوانُ لَوْ كانُوا يَعْلَمُونَ» **"The life of this world is nothing but diversion and play, but the abode of the Hereafter is indeed Life (الحيوان), had they known."** (29:64). And He said: «فَأَعْرِضْ عَنْ مَنْ تَوَلَّى عَنْ ذِكْرِنا وَلَمْ يُرِدْ إِلَّا الْحَياةَ الدُّنْيا ذلِكَ مَبْلَغُهُمْ مِنَ الْعِلْمِ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ هُوَ أَعْلَمُ بِمَنْ ضَلَّ عَنْ سَبِيلِهِ وَهُوَ أَعْلَمُ بِمَنِ اهْتَدى» **"So avoid those who turn away from Our remembrance and desire nothing but the life of the world. That is the ultimate reach of their knowledge. Indeed your Lord knows best those who stray from His way, and He knows best those who are guided."** (53:29-30).

So the Muslim must believe in his Lord and be raised by His training, resolve with His resolve, and gather his desire upon what is with his Lord. For he is but a managed servant who possesses neither harm nor benefit, nor death nor life nor resurrection. Whoever has this description has no business except with his Lord, in Whose hand is good and evil, benefit and harm, wealth and poverty, death and life. It is upon him to travel the path of life with beneficial knowledge and righteous deeds. Whatever worldly advantages he is blessed with are a gift from his Lord, and whatever he is deprived of, he seeks his reward with his Lord; and what is with Allah is better and more lasting.

This is not in any way a nullification of causes, nor an invalidation of the human primordial nature (الفطرة) that calls to work and acquisition, summons to the use of thought and will, and incites to diligence in organizing factors and causes that lead to human goals and correct vital purposes. We have detailed the explanation of this in various places in this book.

When the Muslims follow this divine way (السنة الإلهية) and turn the desire of their hearts from that material enjoyment—which is nothing but an animalistic goal and a material purpose—to this spiritual enjoyment in which there is no competition or deprivation, enmity and hatred will be lifted from their hearts. Their souls will be purified from stinginess and rust, and they will become, by the grace of Allah, brothers, and they will achieve true success. He said: «يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا اتَّقُوا اللهَ حَقَّ تُقاتِهِ وَلا تَمُوتُنَّ إِلَّا وَأَنْتُمْ مُسْلِمُونَ وَاعْتَصِمُوا بِحَبْلِ اللهِ جَمِيعاً وَلا تَفَرَّقُوا وَاذْكُرُوا نِعْمَتَ اللهِ عَلَيْكُمْ إِذْ كُنْتُمْ أَعْداءً فَأَلَّفَ بَيْنَ قُلُوبِكُمْ فَأَصْبَحْتُمْ بِنِعْمَتِهِ إِخْواناً» **"O you who have faith! Be wary of Allah with the wariness due to Him, and do not die except as Muslims. Hold fast, all together, to Allah’s cord, and do not be divided [into sects]. And remember Allah’s blessing upon you when you were enemies, then He united your hearts, so you became brothers by His blessing..."** (3:102-103). And He said: «وَمَنْ يُوقَ شُحَّ نَفْسِهِ فَأُولئِكَ هُمُ الْمُفْلِحُونَ» **"and those who are saved from their own greed—it is they who are the felicitous."** (59:9).

**His saying, the Exalted:** «يا أَيُّهَا النَّبِيُّ حَسْبُكَ اللهُ وَمَنِ اتَّبَعَكَ مِنَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ» **"O Prophet! Allah is sufficient for you and those who follow you of the faithful."** (8:64). This is a comforting of the Prophet's (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) soul. The Exalted had said before it: "then Allah is indeed sufficient for you. It is He who supported you with His help and with the faithful." So the meaning—and Allah knows best—is: Allah suffices you with His help and with those who follow you of the believers. It does not mean that there are two sufficient causes, or a sufficient cause with two parts that form a single sufficient cause; for Quranic monotheism (التوحيد) — *the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts* — rejects that.

It has been said that the meaning is: Allah is sufficient for you and sufficient for those who follow you of the believers, by coordinating His saying "those who follow you" (مَنِ اتَّبَعَكَ) with the position of the "ka" (ك) in "hasbuka" (حَسْبُكَ).

In any case, the discourse is aimed at inciting to fight, as indicated by the context and external evidences. For the effect of the believers in their sufficiency for him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) is primarily in fighting, as it first comes to mind.

Some have mentioned that the verse was revealed at al-Bayda’ before the Battle of Badr. Based on this, it has no connection to what follows it, and its connection to what precedes it is not certain.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «يا أَيُّهَا النَّبِيُّ حَرِّضِ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ عَلَى الْقِتالِ» **"O Prophet! Rouse the faithful to fighting..."** (8:65) to the end of the verse. Incitement (التحريض), urging (التحضيض), encouraging (الترغيب), prompting (الحض), and spurring (الحث) are all in the same sense. **al-Fiqh** (الفقه) is more profound and abundant than "al-fahm" (understanding). His saying: «إِنْ يَكُنْ مِنْكُمْ عِشْرُونَ صابِرُونَ يَغْلِبُوا مِائَتَيْنِ» **"If there be twenty of you who are patient, they shall overcome two hundred"** means from those who are faithless, as the "thousand" was later qualified by it. Likewise, His saying: «وَإِنْ يَكُنْ مِنْكُمْ مِائَةٌ» means a hundred patient ones, as the "twenty" was qualified by it before.

His saying: «بِأَنَّهُمْ قَوْمٌ لا يَفْقَهُونَ» **"because they are a people who do not understand."** (8:65). The "ba" (ب) is for causality (السببية) or instrument (الآلة), and the sentence is justificatory, connected to His saying: "they shall overcome." That is, twenty patient ones from among you will overcome two hundred of those who are faithless, and a hundred patient ones from among you will overcome a thousand of those who are faithless—all of that because the disbelievers are a people who do not understand.

The lack of understanding (الفقه) in the disbelievers, and by contrast its presence in the believers, is what necessitated that one of the twenty believers should outweigh more than ten of the two hundred disbelievers, such that the twenty of the former overcome the two hundred of the latter, according to the basis of the ruling in the verse. For the believers only advance in what they advance based on faith in Allah, which is the power that no other power can equal or resist, because it is built upon correct understanding (الفقه الصحيح). This understanding describes them with every virtuous psychological trait, such as courage, gallantry, boldness, steadfastness, dignity, tranquility, trust in Allah, and the certainty that they are upon one of the two best things: if they kill, they are in Paradise, and if they are killed, they are in Paradise. And that death, in the sense seen by the disbelievers—which is annihilation (الفناء)—has no reality.

As for the disbelievers, their reliance is only upon the whims of the soul (هوى النفس), and their dependence is upon the outward appearance of what Satan embellishes for them. Souls that depend on their whims do not agree upon a goal, and even if they occasionally agree, they only persist in it as long as the specter of death—which they see as annihilation—does not appear. How rare it is for a soul to remain firm upon its whim even when threatened with death while maintaining steadfastness of thought! Rather, it inclines with the slightest contrary wind, especially in general fears and comprehensive terrors. This was proven by history in the defeat of the polytheists on the day of Badr, when they were a thousand, due to the killing of seventy of them. The ratio of seventy to a thousand is close to the ratio of one to fourteen. Thus, their defeat was in the sense of the defeat of fourteen fighters by one fighter. This is only due to the understanding (فقه) of the believers, which is accompanied by knowledge and faith, and the ignorance of the disbelievers, which is accompanied by disbelief and whim.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «الْآنَ خَفَّفَ اللهُ عَنْكُمْ وَعَلِمَ أَنَّ فِيكُمْ ضَعْفاً فَإِنْ يَكُنْ» **"Now Allah has lightened it for you, and He knew that there is a weakness in you. So if there be..."** (8:66) etc. That is, if there be a hundred patient ones from among you, they shall overcome two hundred of those who are faithless; and if there be a thousand patient ones from among you, they shall overcome two hundred of those who are faithless, following the pattern of what preceded in the previous verse.

His saying: "and He knew that there is a weakness in you" (وَعَلِمَ أَنَّ فِيكُمْ ضَعْفاً) refers to weakness in spiritual attributes, which inevitably leads back to faith. For certainty in the truth (الإيقان بالحق) is that from which all good traits arise that necessitate victory and success, such as courage, patience, and sound judgment. As for weakness in terms of numbers and equipment, it is a matter of necessity that the believers never ceased to increase in numbers and strength during the time of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله).

His saying: "by Allah’s leave" (بِإِذْنِ اللهِ) is a qualification of His saying: "they shall overcome." That is, Allah does not will otherwise while you are patient believers. By this, it becomes clear that His saying: "and Allah is with the patient" (وَاللهُ مَعَ الصَّابِرِينَ) provides the benefit of justification (التعليل) with respect to the "leave."

The saying of the Exalted in the previous verse as a justification for the ruling: "because they are a people who do not understand," and likewise in this verse: "and He knew that there is a weakness in you" and "and Allah is with the patient"—and the lack of understanding, spiritual weakness, and patience are among the external causes and factors that influence victory, success, and triumph—undoubtedly indicates that the ruling in the two verses is built upon what was considered of spiritual attributes in the two groups: the believers and the disbelievers. The internal spiritual powers that were considered in the first verse—where those in one believer were dominant over the internal spiritual powers in ten of the disbelievers—returned after a short time, indicated by His saying: "Now Allah has lightened it for you," such that those in one believer—from among the average believers—do not outweigh more than two of the disbelievers. Thus, the power lost its effect by a ratio of eighty percent, and the twenty and two hundred in the first verse changed to the hundred and two hundred in the second verse, and the hundred and thousand in the first to the thousand and two thousand in the second.

Careful investigation into the factors that generate psychological traits according to the conditions that befall man in societies leads to this. For domestic societies and parties formed for the sake of a vital goal—whether worldly or religious—at their first formation and inception, feel the opposing obstacles and the trials that destroy their foundation from every side. Thus, their defensive powers are alerted to struggle (الجهاد) for their goal, which they deem legitimate, and their dormant psychological states awaken to guard against hardships and to sacrifice wealth and soul in the path of their objective.

They continue to struggle and sacrifice night and day, growing stronger and advancing until they establish for themselves a life with some independence. The atmosphere clears for them somewhat, their numbers increase, and they become firmly established on the earth. Then they begin to benefit from the fruits of their effort and enjoy the blessing of rest and the expansion of security. At that point, the spiritual powers that were expansive and inciting to action begin to subside.

Furthermore, a society, even if its members are few, is not free from differences in faith and beautiful spiritual traits, between the strong and the weak. Whenever the individuals increase, the weak in faith, those in whose hearts is a disease, and the hypocrites increase. Thus, the spiritual powers in the average individual decline, and the scale of the balance rises from the weight it once had.

Religious groups and worldly parties are equal in this regard. The natural way (السنة الطبيعية) that proceeds in the human system applies to all in the same manner. Definitive experience has proven that united societies for an important goal, whenever their members are few and their rivals and competitors are strong, and trials and tribulations surround them, they are more active in work and sharper in effect. And whenever their members increase and their competitors and the obstacles between them and their goals and objectives decrease, they are more subsided, less vigilant, and more foolish in judgment.

Sufficient reflection on the military expeditions (مغازي) of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) illuminates this. This is the Battle of Badr, in which the Muslims—who were three hundred and some odd men, despite their poor condition, lack of equipment, and absence of weapons and power—overcame the disbelievers of the Quraysh, who were three times the number of the Muslims or more, despite their pride, might, and power. Then there is what happened to the Muslims in the Battle of Uhud, then in the Battle of the Trench (الخندق), then in the Battle of Khaybar, then in the Battle of Hunayn—which is the most amazing of them. Allah, the Glorified, mentioned it in a way that leaves no doubt for a researcher, as He said: «وَيَوْمَ حُنَيْنٍ إِذْ أَعْجَبَتْكُمْ كَثْرَتُكُمْ فَلَمْ تُغْنِ عَنْكُمْ شَيْئاً وَضاقَتْ عَلَيْكُمُ الْأَرْضُ بِما رَحُبَتْ ثُمَّ وَلَّيْتُمْ مُدْبِرِينَ» **"and on the day of Hunayn, when your great number tickled your fancy, but did not avail you anything, and the earth became narrow for you despite its expanse; then you turned your backs [to flee]."** (9:25) to the end of the verses.

The verse indicates, firstly, that as Islam increased in outward might and power during the time of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), it increased in inward deficiency and subsidence in the general spiritual powers of the Muslims and the degree of their faith and beautiful, spiritual, internal traits. This continued until it settled after the Battle of Badr—by a little or a lot—at one-fifth of what it was before it, as indicated by some allusion in the saying of the Exalted in the following verses: «ما كانَ لِنَبِيٍّ أَنْ يَكُونَ لَهُ أَسْرى حَتَّى يُثْخِنَ فِي الْأَرْضِ تُرِيدُونَ عَرَضَ الدُّنْيا وَاللهُ يُرِيدُ الْآخِرَةَ وَاللهُ عَزِيزٌ حَكِيمٌ لَوْ لا كِتابٌ مِنَ اللهِ سَبَقَ لَمَسَّكُمْ فِيما أَخَذْتُمْ عَذابٌ عَظِيمٌ» **"A prophet may not take captives until he has fully established his hold on the land. You desire the transitory goods of this world, while Allah desires the Hereafter [for you], and Allah is All-mighty, All-wise. Had it not been for a prior decree from Allah, there would have surely befallen you a great punishment for what you took."** (8:67-68).

Secondly: It appears that the two verses were revealed at once. For although they inform about the condition of the believers in two different times—as indicated by His saying in the second verse: "Now Allah has lightened it for you"—the two verses compare, as mentioned, the nature of the believers' spiritual powers in two different times. The context of the second, in view of this comparison, is such that it is not independent of the first. The existence of two different rulings in two times does not necessitate that the verse containing one of them was revealed at a time other than the time of the revelation of the other containing the second.

Yes, if the two verses were restricted to clarifying the legislative ruling (الحكم التكليفي) only, it would appear that the second was revealed after a time in which the first was revealed.

Thirdly: The appearance of His saying, the Exalted: "Now Allah has lightened it for you"—as has been said—is that the two verses are aimed at clarifying the legislative ruling, because lightening (التخفيف) only occurs after an obligation (التكليف). Thus, the wording is that of a report (خبر), but the intended meaning is a command (أمر). The gist of the meaning in the first verse is: let one of you stand firm against ten of the disbelievers; and in the second verse: now Allah has lightened His command, so let one of you stand firm against two of the disbelievers.

The restriction of "lightening" to the chapter of obligations—as has been said—although it can be debated, the appearance of the two verses in the existence of two different rulings, sequential in time, one of which is lighter than the other, should not be doubted.

Fourthly: The appearance of the justification in the first verse by "understanding" (الفقه), and in the second verse by "patience" (الصبر), while qualifying the fighter from among the believers in both verses with patience, indicates that patience outweighs the one in spiritual power over two like him, and understanding outweighs him in it over five like him. So when they meet in one person, he outweighs ten times himself. Patience does not separate from understanding, although the reverse is possible.

Fifthly: Patience is obligatory in fighting in any case.

## (Discussion of Narrations)

In the *Tafsir* of al-Baydawi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: «الَّذِينَ عاهَدْتَ مِنْهُمْ ثُمَّ يَنْقُضُونَ عَهْدَهُمْ فِي كُلِّ مَرَّةٍ» **"Those with whom you made a treaty, and who then break their treaty every time"**: They are the Jews of Banu Qurayzah. The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) made a treaty with them that they would not aid anyone against him, but they aided the polytheists with weapons—and said: "We forgot." Then he made a treaty with them, and they broke it—and aided them against him on the Day of the Trench (الخندق). Ka‘b ibn al-Ashraf rode to Mecca and allied with them.

**I say:** This has been narrated from Ibn ‘Abbas and Mujahid. It is narrated from Sa‘id ibn Jubayr that the verse was revealed concerning six individuals from the Jews, among them Ibn Tabut. The clarification of what the verse alludes to regarding the Jews' breaking of the Prophet's (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) covenant time after time, and the hardships he endured from their side, requires a general survey of what occurred between him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and them from the time of his migration (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to Medina until seven years after the Hijrah.

Groups of Jews had migrated from their lands to the Hijaz and settled there, building forts and castles. Their numbers increased, their wealth grew, and their affair became significant. Narrations regarding the beginning of their migration to the Hijaz, how they settled around Medina, and their giving glad tidings to the people of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) have already been mentioned under His saying, the Exalted: «وَلَمَّا جاءَهُمْ كِتابٌ مِنْ عِنْدِ اللهِ مُصَدِّقٌ لِما مَعَهُمْ وَكانُوا مِنْ قَبْلُ يَسْتَفْتِحُونَ عَلَى الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا فَلَمَّا جاءَهُمْ ما عَرَفُوا كَفَرُوا بِهِ فَلَعْنَةُ اللهِ عَلَى الْكافِرِينَ» **"And when there came to them a Book from Allah, confirming what was with them—and earlier they would pray for victory over the faithless—so when there came to them what they recognized, they denied it. So may Allah’s curse be on the faithless!"** (2:89) in the first volume of the book.

When the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) migrated to Medina and called them to Islam, they disdained to believe in him. So he made peace with the Jews of Medina and entered into a treaty with them through a document written between him and them. They were three groups around Medina: Banu Qaynuqa‘, Banu al-Nadir, and Banu Qurayzah. As for Banu Qaynuqa‘, they broke the covenant during the Battle of Badr. The Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) marched to them in the middle of Shawwal in the second year of the Hijrah, twenty-some days after the event of Badr. They took refuge in their forts, so he besieged them with a severe siege, and they remained so for fifteen days.

Then they surrendered to the judgment of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) regarding their lives, wealth, women, and progeny. He ordered them to be bound. ‘Abdullah ibn Ubayy ibn Salul spoke to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) regarding them and persisted, as they were his allies. So he granted them to him and ordered them to leave Medina and not to be his neighbors there. They left for Adhri‘at in Syria, with their women and progeny, and he seized their wealth as spoils of war (غنيمة الحرب). They were six hundred fighters from the bravest of the Jews.

As for Banu al-Nadir, they plotted against the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) when he went out to them with a group of his companions months after the Battle of Badr. He spoke to them to assist him in the blood money (دية) of a group or two men from the Kilabites whom ‘Amr ibn Umayyah al-Damri had killed. They said: "We will do so, O Abu al-Qasim. Sit here until we fulfill your need." They went into private council and conspired to kill him. They chose from among them ‘Amr ibn Juhash to take a millstone, climb up, and throw it on his head to crush it. Sallam ibn Mishkam warned them, saying: "Do not do that! By Allah, he will surely be informed of what you have intended, and it is a violation of the covenant between us and him."

Revelation came to him, and his Lord informed him of what they had intended. So he (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) rose from his seat quickly and headed toward Medina. His companions followed him and inquired about his rising and departure. He informed them of what Banu al-Nadir had intended. He sent word to them from Medina: "Leave Medina and do not live with me in it. I have given you a grace period; whoever I find after that among you, I will strike his neck." They stayed for days preparing to leave.

The hypocrite ‘Abdullah ibn Ubayy sent word to them: "Do not leave your homes, for I have two thousand who will enter your fort with you and die for you. Banu Qurayzah and your allies from Ghatafan will help you." He satisfied them with that.

So their leader, Huyayy ibn Akhtab, sent word to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) saying: "We will not leave our homes, so do what you see fit." The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said "Allahu Akbar" and his companions said "Allahu Akbar." He ordered ‘Ali (عليه‌السلام) to carry the banner and march to them. They marched and surrounded their homes. ‘Abdullah ibn Ubayy betrayed them, and neither Banu Qurayzah nor their allies from Ghatafan helped them.

The Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) had ordered the cutting and burning of their palm trees, which caused them great distress. They said: "O Muhammad, do not cut them; if they are yours, then take them, and if they are ours, then leave them for us." After a few days, they said to him: "O Muhammad, we will leave your land, so give us our wealth." He replied: "No, but you shall leave and take only what your camels can carry." They refused this and remained as they were for some days. Later, they relented and asked him for that same deal, but he said: "No, but you shall leave and none of you shall carry anything; whomever we find with anything of it, we shall kill him." So they departed; some went to Fadak and Wadi al-Qura, while others went to the land of ash-Sham. Their wealth became spoils (فيئاً) for Allah and His Messenger without the Muslim army having to strike a blow for it. Their story is mentioned in Surah al-Hashr. Among the plots of Banu al-Nadir against the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) was the mobilization of the Confederates (الأحزاب) from Quraysh, Ghatafan, and others against him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله).

As for Banu Qurayzah, they remained in a state of truce and peace until the Battle of the Trench (غزوة الخندق) occurred. Huyayy ibn Akhtab, the chief of Banu al-Nadir, had traveled to Makkah and incited Quraysh against the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and organized the Confederates. During that time, he rode to Banu Qurayzah and came to them in their homes. He continued whispering to them, encouraging them, and persisting until he spoke to their chief, Ka'b ibn Asad, regarding the matter and the breaking of the covenant and engaging the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) in battle, until he satisfied them with that. They stipulated that he must enter their fortress so that whatever befell them would befall him; he accepted and entered.

Thus, they broke the covenant and leaned toward the Confederates who were besieging Madinah. They openly insulted the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and created another breach.

When the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) finished with the matter of the Confederates, Gabriel came to him with a revelation from Allah commanding him to march against them. He set out toward them, with Ali (عليه‌السلام) carrying his banner, and besieged the fortresses of Banu Qurayzah for twenty-five days.

When the siege became severe for them, their chief, Ka'b ibn Asad, proposed that they choose one of three options: either they embrace Islam and enter the religion of Muhammad; or they kill their children and go out to him with their swords drawn to fight him until they either triumph or are killed to the last man; or they launch a surprise attack on him on the Sabbath, because they—meaning the Muslims—would feel secure that they would not be fought on that day.

They refused to agree to any of these options. They sent word to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) asking him to send Abu Lubabah ibn Abd al-Mundhir so they could consult him on the matter; Abu Lubabah was a sincere advisor to them because his family, children, and wealth were with them.

He sent him to them. When they saw him, they stood up to him weeping and said: "How do you see our surrendering to the judgment of Muhammad?" He said: "Yes," and pointed with his hand to his throat, indicating slaughter. Abu Lubabah said: "By Allah, my feet had not moved before I realized that I had betrayed Allah and His Messenger." Allah then sent revelation to His Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) regarding the matter of Abu Lubabah.

Abu Lubabah felt deep remorse and went straight to the Mosque, where he tied himself to one of its pillars, repenting to Allah. He swore that no one should untie him except the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) or he would die. When this reached the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), he said: "Leave him until Allah accepts his repentance." Subsequently, Allah accepted his repentance and revealed his forgiveness, and the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) untied him.

Thereafter, Banu Qurayzah surrendered to the judgment of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). They were allies of the Aws tribe, so the Aws spoke to him regarding them, seeking intercession (مستشفعين). The matter concluded with the appointment of Sa'd ibn Mu'adh al-Awsi to judge their case. They agreed to this, and the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) also agreed. Sa'd was brought forth while he was wounded.

When Sa'd (رحمه الله) was spoken to regarding their matter, he said: "The time has come for Sa'd that he should not be affected, for the sake of Allah, by the blame of any blamer." He then judged that the men be killed, the women and children be taken captive, and the wealth be seized. What Sa'd judged was carried out upon them; their necks were struck to the last man. They were six hundred or seven hundred fighters, and it is said more. None of them survived except a few who believed before their execution. Amr ibn Su'da escaped from them, as he had not joined them in breaking the covenant. The women were taken captive except for one woman who was executed; she was the one who had thrown a millstone onto the head of Khallad ibn al-Suwayd ibn al-Samit (رحمه الله), killing him.

Then the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) expelled those Jews who remained in Madinah. Afterward, he (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) marched toward the Jews of Khaybar due to their plotting and their efforts in inciting the Confederates against him and their assembling of all the Arab tribes to fight him. He besieged their fortresses for several days. The Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) sent Abu Bakr with a group one day to fight them, but he was defeated; then he sent Umar ibn al-Khattab with a group another day, and he was defeated.

At that point, the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: "Tomorrow I shall certainly give the banner to a man who loves Allah and His Messenger—and whom Allah and His Messenger love—a relentless attacker who never flees; he shall not return until Allah grants victory at his hands." On the following day, he gave the banner to Ali (عليه‌السلام) and sent him to fight the people. He advanced toward them and killed Marhab, their famous knight, and defeated them. He wrenched off the gate of their fortress with his hand, and Allah granted victory at his hands. This occurred after the Truce of al-Hudaybiyyah in Muharram of the seventh year of the Hijrah.

Then the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) expelled the remaining Jews, having advised them before that to sell their properties and take their prices. This concludes what we intended to summarize regarding the story of the Jews with the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله).

In *Tafsir al-Ayyashi*, narrated from Jabir: regarding His saying, the Exalted: **إِنَّ شَرَّ الدَّوَابِّ عِنْدَ اللهِ** "Indeed the worst of beasts in the sight of Allah..." [Qarai 8:55]—the verse was revealed concerning the Umayyads; they are the worst of Allah's creation. They are **الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا** "those who disbelieve" in the inner meaning (باطن) of the Quran, and they are **الَّذِينَ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ** "those who do not believe."

**I say:** Al-Qummi narrated something similar from Abu Hamzah from him (عليه‌السلام), and it is from the inner meaning (باطن) of the Quran as explicitly stated in the narration, not the apparent meaning (الظاهر).

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain of narration from Sahl ibn Ziyad from some of his companions from Abdullah ibn Sinan from Abu Abdillah (عليه‌السلام), who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'Three traits, if found in a person, make him a hypocrite—even if he fasts, prays, and claims to be a Muslim: he who betrays when entrusted, lies when he speaks, and breaks his word when he promises.' Indeed, Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, said in His Book: **إِنَّ اللهَ لا يُحِبُّ الْخائِنِينَ** 'Indeed Allah does not like the treacherous' [Qarai 8:58], and He said: **أَنَّ لَعْنَتَ اللهِ عَلَيْهِ إِنْ كانَ مِنَ الْكاذِبِينَ** '...that the curse of Allah be upon him if he is one of the liars' [Qarai 24:7]. And regarding His saying, the Mighty and Majestic: **وَاذْكُرْ فِي الْكِتابِ إِسْماعِيلَ ـ إِنَّهُ كانَ صادِقَ الْوَعْدِ وَكانَ رَسُولاً نَبِيًّا** 'And mention in the Book Ishmael. Indeed he was true to his promise, and he was an apostle and a prophet' [Qarai 19:54]."

In *Tafsir al-Qummi*: regarding His saying, the Exalted: **وَأَعِدُّوا لَهُمْ مَا اسْتَطَعْتُمْ مِنْ قُوَّةٍ** "Prepare against them whatever you can of [military] power..." [Qarai 8:60]—he said: "He (عليه‌السلام) said: 'Weapons (السلاح).'"

In *Tafsir al-Ayyashi*, from Muhammad ibn Isa from someone who mentioned it from Abu Abdillah (عليه‌السلام): regarding the verse, he said: "A sword and a shield."

In *al-Faqih*, from al-Sadiq (عليه‌السلام) in a detached (مرسلاً) narration: regarding the verse, he said: "Included in it is dyeing [the hair] with black."

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain of narration from Jabir from Abu Ja'far (عليه‌السلام): A group of people entered upon al-Husayn ibn Ali (عليه‌السلام) and saw him dyed with black. They asked him about that, so he extended his hand to his beard and said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) commanded in a battle he fought that they should dye [their hair] with black, so they might appear stronger against the polytheists."

In *Tafsir al-Ayyashi*, from Jabir al-Ansari, who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said regarding: **وَأَعِدُّوا لَهُمْ مَا اسْتَطَعْتُمْ مِنْ قُوَّةٍ** 'Prepare against them whatever you can of [military] power'—'It is archery (الرمي).'"

**I say:** It was narrated in *al-Kafi* with its chain from Abdullah ibn al-Mughirah in a raised (رفعه) report from him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله); by al-Zamakhshari in *Rabi' al-Abrar* from Uqbah ibn Amir from him; and by al-Suyuti in *al-Durr al-Manthur* from Ahmad, Muslim, Abu Dawud, Ibn Majah, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, Abu al-Shaykh, Abu Ya'qub Ishaq ibn Ibrahim, and al-Bayhaqi from Uqbah ibn Amir al-Juhani from him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله).

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Abu Dawud, al-Tirmidhi, Ibn Majah, al-Hakim (who authenticated it), and al-Bayhaqi extracted from Uqbah ibn Amir al-Juhani (رضي الله عنه), who said: "I heard the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) saying: 'Indeed, Allah admits three people into Paradise because of a single arrow: its maker who seeks reward in his craftsmanship, the one who prepares it in the way of Allah, and the one who shoots it in the way of Allah.'"

And he said: "Shoot and ride, and that you shoot is better than that you ride." And he said: "Everything with which a son of Adam amuses himself is vain except three: his shooting with his bow, his training of his horse, and his playing with his wife, for these are from the truth. And whoever learns archery and then abandons it, it is a blessing (نعمة) that he has been ungrateful for."

**I say:** There are other narrations with these meanings, especially regarding horses and archery. In any case, the narrations are by way of enumerating instances (المصاديق).

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Sa'd, al-Harith ibn Abi Usamah, Abu Ya'la, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, Ibn Qani' in his *Mu'jam*, al-Tabarani, Abu al-Shaykh, Ibn Mandah, al-Ruyani in his *Musnad*, Ibn Mardawayh, and Ibn Asakir extracted from Yazid ibn Abdullah ibn Gharib from his father from his grandfather from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) regarding His saying: **وَآخَرِينَ مِنْ دُونِهِمْ لا تَعْلَمُونَهُمُ اللهُ يَعْلَمُهُمْ** "...and others besides them, whom you do not know [but] whom Allah knows" [Qarai 8:60]—he said: "They are the Jinn; and Satan does not afflict a human with madness in a house where there is a noble horse."

**I say:** There are other narrations with this meaning. The gist of these narrations is to link the saying: **وَآخَرِينَ مِنْ دُونِهِمْ لا تَعْلَمُونَهُمُ اللهُ يَعْلَمُهُمْ** with the saying: **وَمِنْ رِباطِ الْخَيْلِ** "and of steeds of war." This is of the nature of application (الجري) and is not part of the formal exegesis (التفسير) at all. The verse, in its apparent meaning (بظاهرها), refers to human enemies such as the disbelievers and the hypocrites.

In the same source, Ibn Mardawayh extracted from Abd al-Rahman ibn Abza that the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) used to recite: **وَإِنْ جَنَحُوا لِلسَّلْمِ** (And if they incline toward peace).

In the same source, Abu Ubayd, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, and Ibn Mardawayh extracted from Ibn Abbas regarding His saying: **وَإِنْ جَنَحُوا لِلسَّلْمِ فَاجْنَحْ لَها** "And if they incline toward peace, then you [too] incline toward it..." [Qarai 8:61]—he said: "This verse was abrogated by the verse: **قاتِلُوا الَّذِينَ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِاللهِ وَلا بِالْيَوْمِ الْآخِرِ** 'Fight those who do not have faith in Allah nor [believe] in the Last Day...' until His saying: **صاغِرُونَ** '...while they are humbled' [Qarai 9:29]."

**I say:** Its abrogation was also narrated by the verse of *Bara'ah*: **فاقْتُلُوا الْمُشْرِكِينَ حَيْثُ وَجَدْتُمُوهُمْ** "kill the polytheists wherever you find them" [Qarai 9:5]. However, the verse is not devoid of an allusion to the ruling being temporary, as He said: **وَإِنْ جَنَحُوا لِلسَّلْمِ فَاجْنَحْ لَها وَتَوَكَّلْ عَلَى اللهِ إِنَّهُ هُوَ السَّمِيعُ الْعَلِيمُ** "And if they incline toward peace, then you [too] incline toward it, and put your trust in Allah. Indeed He is the All-hearing, the All-knowing" [Qarai 8:61].

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain from al-Halabi from Abu Abdillah (عليه‌السلام) regarding His saying, the Exalted: **وَإِنْ جَنَحُوا لِلسَّلْمِ فَاجْنَحْ لَها**—I asked: "What is peace (السلم)?" He said: "Entering into our matter." In another narration: "Entering into your matter."

**I say:** This is of the nature of application (الجري).

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ibn Asakir extracted from Abu Hurayrah, who said: "It is written on the Throne: 'There is no god but I alone, I have no partner; Muhammad is My servant and My Messenger, I aided him through Ali.' And that is His saying: **هُوَ الَّذِي أَيَّدَكَ بِنَصْرِهِ وَبِالْمُؤْمِنِينَ** 'It is He who strengthened you with His help and with the faithful' [Qarai 8:62]."

**I say:** Al-Saduq narrated it in *al-Ma'ani* with his chain from Abu Hurayrah, and Abu Nu'aym in *Hilyat al-Awliya'* with his chain from him, as did Ibn Shahrashub with a chain from Anas from the Prophet (ص).

In *Tafsir al-Burhan*, from Sharaf al-Din al-Najafi, who said: "Its interpretation (تأويله) was mentioned by Abu Nu'aym in *Hilyat al-Awliya'* through his chain from Abu Hurayrah, who said: 'This verse was revealed concerning Ali ibn Abi Talib, and he is the one intended by His saying: **المؤمنين** (the faithful).'"

**I say:** The wording of the verse does not readily support this, unless what is intended by "following" is the complete following from which no aspect of one's affairs deviates, and the word "from" (من) is for partitivity (التبعيض) rather than explanation (البيان), if the context (السياق) allows for it.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, al-Bazzar extracted from Ibn Abbas, who said: "When Umar embraced Islam, the polytheists said: 'The people have gained half of us today,' and Allah revealed: **يا أَيُّهَا النَّبِيُّ حَسْبُكَ اللهُ ـ وَمَنِ اتَّبَعَكَ مِنَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ** 'O Prophet! Allah is sufficient for you and those of the faithful who follow you' [Qarai 8:64]."

**I say:** This meaning was narrated in other reports, but reason (الاعتبار) does not support it. The time in which he embraced Islam was not of a nature that would validate an address such as: **يا أَيُّهَا النَّبِيُّ حَسْبُكَ اللهُ وَمَنِ اتَّبَعَكَ مِنَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ**; those were days of trial and hardship, and that state continued for many long years. The Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) at that time did not need anything to aid him in terms of numbers. In these narrations, it is said that he was the completion of forty or the forty-fourth. Furthermore, it is apparent that the verse is Madinan, being among the verses of Surah al-Anfal.

In the same source, Ibn Ishaq and Ibn Abi Hatim extracted from al-Zuhri regarding His saying: **يا أَيُّهَا النَّبِيُّ حَسْبُكَ اللهُ وَمَنِ اتَّبَعَكَ مِنَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ**—he said: "It was revealed concerning the Ansar."

**I say:** The context (سياق) of the verse does not support this, just as it did not support the previous two narrations, unless what is intended is its revelation on the day the Ansar believed in him or the day they followed him. It appears that the verse was revealed to comfort the heart of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) with all those who were with him of the believers: both the Emigrants (المهاجرين) and the Helpers (الأنصار). It serves as a preamble and preparation for what follows in the next verse regarding the command to urge the believers to fight.

In *Tafsir al-Qummi*, he said: "He said: 'The ruling at the beginning of the Prophethood regarding the companions of the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) was that a single man was obligated to fight ten of the disbelievers; if he fled from them, he was one who fled from the battlefield (الفار من الزحف), and a hundred would fight a thousand.'"

"Then Allah knew that there was weakness in them and they were unable to do that, so Allah revealed: **الْآنَ خَفَّفَ اللهُ عَنْكُمْ وَعَلِمَ أَنَّ فِيكُمْ ضَعْفاً ـ فَإِنْ يَكُنْ مِنْكُمْ مِائَةٌ صابِرَةٌ يَغْلِبُوا مِائَتَيْنِ** 'Now Allah has lightened it for you, and He knew that there is a weakness in you. So if there be a hundred of you, patient and steadfast, they shall overcome two hundred...' [Qarai 8:66]. Thus, He made it obligatory for the least of the believers to fight two of the disbelievers; if he flees from them, he is one who flees from the battlefield. But if there are three disbelievers against one Muslim and the Muslim flees from them, he is not one who flees from the battlefield."

**I say:** In *Tafsir al-Ayyashi*, from al-Husayn ibn Salih from al-Sadiq from Ali (عليه‌السلام), there is something close to this. What is in its meaning was also narrated in *al-Durr al-Manthur* through numerous chains from Ibn Abbas and others.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, al-Shirazi in *al-Alqab*, Ibn Adi, and al-Hakim (who authenticated it) extracted from Ibn Umar: "That the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) recited: **الْآنَ خَفَّفَ اللهُ عَنْكُمْ ـ وَعَلِمَ أَنَّ فِيكُمْ ضَعْفاً** with the word 'weakness' (ضعفاً) pronounced with a *damma* (رفع) [on the *dad*]."
